---
title: Assumptions and limitations
---

This page is the honest accounting of what `tspaint` assumes, where its parameters
can fail to be identified, and the regimes in which it stops working. It is the
companion to the [Overview](overview.qmd): the Overview tells you how to run
the method; this page tells you when to trust the result and when not to. None of
the caveats below are hypothetical hedges — each is either forced by the model or
has been measured directly. The MEASURED numbers are quoted verbatim from the
project's validation runs.

The short version: `tspaint` is a *generative* model — a continuous-time Markov
chain (CTMC) for an ancestry character riding up the branches of an inferred tree
sequence, fitted by EM. Generative models give you calibrated soft posteriors and
an interpretable, readable `Q`, but they also have identifiability structure that
a black-box classifier hides. The sharp edges here are the price of that
interpretability.

## 1. Input requirement: cross-tree node-ID stability {#sec-input}

::: {.callout-warning}
## The premise is delivered by the *input*, not the method

`tspaint` accumulates its sufficient statistics **per tree-sequence edge,
span-weighted** (the lead novelty, CLAUDE.md §3.3). This is what removes
double-counting and is the channel through which genome-scale autocorrelation
enters: a clade that persists across many marginal trees must be **one set of
edges with a wide span**, identified by a stable node ID across those trees. If
the input does not deliver that stability, the central premise is not met — the
method is still *correct*, but it is no better than independent per-tree pruning.
:::

Two front ends deliver the persistence invariant:

- **Relate → tskit with `--compress`.** The `--compress` flag (Nathaniel S. Pope)
  assigns the same age to nodes with **identical descendant sets across adjacent
  trees**, unifying a persistent subclade into a single long-span node. This is
  not optional. Without it, Relate's conversion can mint fresh node IDs per local
  tree even for unchanged descendant sets, producing short churning edges that
  retain the double-counting fix but lose almost all autocorrelation benefit.
- **`tsinfer` / `tsdate`** tree sequences have native cross-tree node-ID stability
  by construction and are an equally valid (or preferable) front end.

Two source-confirmed caveats on `--compress` ([Speidel et al., 2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41588-019-0484-x);
`relate_lib`):

1. It unifies on **exact sorted-leaf-set equality, no threshold**
   (`tree_sequence.hpp::FindIdenticalNodes`) — provably stricter than Relate's own
   "equivalent branch" notion (Pearson correlation ≥ 0.9). A clade that persists
   but gains or loses a single tip at a breakpoint is **split** into separate node
   IDs. Autocorrelation capture is therefore slightly **conservative** (the safe
   direction: it never falsely merges distinct lineages, only under-links
   near-equivalent ones).
2. The global root is deliberately re-IDed whenever either of its children
   changes (to avoid forcing a genome-wide constant TMRCA), and merged node ages
   are span-weighted-averaged then monotonicity-constrained. We skip root branches
   anyway (see [§4](#sec-mugration)), so the first is benign; the second compounds
   the time-calibration concern of [§3](#sec-calibration).

### 1.1 The persistence go/no-go {#sec-persistence}

Before any inference, run the persistence check (CLAUDE.md §5.1). It is the
go/no-go test on the method's premise — the histogram of how many distinct
marginal trees each internal node survives:

```python
import tskit, numpy as np
from collections import Counter

ts = tskit.load("example.trees")

# how many distinct trees each internal node survives <-- THE key histogram
node_tree_count = Counter()
for (_interval, eout, ein), tree in zip(ts.edge_diffs(), ts.trees()):
    for u in tree.nodes():
        if not tree.is_sample(u):
            node_tree_count[u] += 1
counts = np.array(list(node_tree_count.values()))
print("internal-node persistence: median", np.median(counts),
      "max", counts.max(), "frac==1", np.mean(counts == 1))
```

- If `frac==1` is near 1 (persistence spiked at a single tree), `--compress`
  did not take or something upstream is wrong — **the method's premise is not
  met. Stop and fix the input.**
- If there is real mass above 1, the horizontal coupling is getting signal.
  Proceed.

The packaged helper `diagnostics.persistence_summary` computes the same histogram.

## 2. Identifiability & collapse modes {#sec-collapse}

The model is well-pinned with a hard-clamped reference panel. The identifiability
problems appear only once labels are allowed to move — i.e. once per-tip
credibility $w_i$ is learned (the soft-reference / introgression-mapping mode,
CLAUDE.md §2.2). The danger is structural: when a reference's genealogical context
disagrees with its label, the model has **three ways** to explain the same
disagreement, and they trade off against each other.

| Collapse mode | What the model does | Why it is degenerate | Guard |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Lower $w_i$** | Declares this haplotype admixed and stops trusting its label | Any disagreement can be absorbed tip-by-tip; the panel dissolves | Hard-clamped anchor set ($w_i \equiv 1$); only the rest float |
| **Inflate $Q$** | Says *everyone* switches ancestry fast | Large $Q\cdot t$ washes every branch toward $\pi$; tips stop informing their ancestors | Informative $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ prior near 1 keeps labels believed |
| **Relabel a clade** | Flips the ancestry assignment of a whole subtree | Pure label-switching: the likelihood is invariant under a global relabel | Never let the whole panel go soft simultaneously |

The implementation enforces the anchor guard directly: [`fit`](../api/fit.html)
raises `ValueError` if `soft_refs` covers every labelled tip, with the message
"keep a hard-clamped anchor set; never let the whole panel float (CLAUDE.md §6)".

::: {.callout-note}
## Two degenerate fixed points to watch in tests

- $Q \to 0$ **freezes the initialization**: with no migration the up-pass never
  mixes states, so whatever the tips say propagates unchanged and EM never moves
  off its starting point.
- $Q \to \infty$ **washes everything to $\pi$**: every branch becomes the
  stationary distribution regardless of its tips, so all posteriors relax to the
  prior and all discriminating signal is lost.

Both are exercised by the degenerate-case tests (`test_em_degenerate.py`). They
are also the two ends of the $Q$-inflation collapse above.
:::

## 3. $\pi$-identifiability — the measured real failure mode {#sec-pi}

::: {.callout-warning}
## When deep branches wash, $\pi$ drifts and the painting collapses to one colour — *confidently*

This is not a hypothetical. It is the failure mode that the order-only ablation
([§3.1](#sec-calibration)) surfaced and that the head-to-head harness caught on
short genomes. **Hold $\pi$ fixed.**
:::

The root frequencies $\pi$ are a prior on the state of the genealogical MRCA. The
M-step estimate is

$$
\pi = \frac{S_{\text{root}}}{\sum_s S_{\text{root}}[s]}
$$ {#eq-pi-mstep}

where $S_{\text{root}}$ is the span-weighted expected root-state mass pooled over
all roots ([`m_step_pi`](../api/em.m_step_pi.html)). The problem: when the
deep/root branches **wash** — large $Q\cdot t$, as happens on sparse or short
ARGs, or under the order-only variant — each tree's root marginal just **echoes
$\pi$** rather than carrying independent evidence about it. So @eq-pi-mstep feeds
$\pi$ back onto itself, $\pi$ is under-determined, and the M-step drifts it to a
degenerate extreme (e.g. $\pi \approx [0.96, 0.04]$). With one state dominating
the prior, every query relaxes toward that state, and the painting **collapses to
one colour with high confidence** — confidently wrong.

**The fix: hold $\pi$ fixed** (`estimate_pi=False`, the
[`tspaint_paint`](../api/compare.tspaint_paint.html) default). Uniform $\pi$ is
*principled*, not a kludge: $\pi$ is a prior on the **arbitrary** GMRCA state, so
uniform is the right non-informative choice. It is estimating $\pi$ from washing
roots that breaks. The lower-level [`fit`](../api/fit.html) exposes
`estimate_pi=True` only for deliberate $\pi$-recovery studies, where deep branches
are well-resolved and $\pi$ *is* identified.

MEASURED recovery numbers (CLAUDE.md §6, §9):

| Regime | `estimate_pi=True` (balanced acc.) | `estimate_pi=False` |
|:--|:--|:--|
| true ARG, order-only (ranked) | 0.50 | 0.94 |
| tsinfer, $L = 5\times10^4$ | 0.50 | **1.00** |
| tsinfer, $L = 2\times10^5$ (good data) | 0.984 | 0.999 |

The fix recovers both failures and is **harmless on good, long data** (the
$L=2\times10^5$ row, where $\pi$ *is* identified). The short/sparse regime is
itself an edge case — real LAI runs on long genomes with many roots, where $\pi$
is identified and `tspaint` already paints 0.98–1.0 — so holding $\pi$ fixed is a
free robustness default, not a patch over a practical limitation.

## 4. Branch-length / time-calibration risk {#sec-calibration}

The CTMC rides on branch lengths through $P(t) = \exp(Q\,t)$, so $Q$ depends on
calibrated node times. Relate estimates branch lengths under a **panmictic
coalescent prior** — misspecified *precisely* in the structured / admixed regime
that LAI targets ([Speidel et al., 2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41588-019-0484-x),
Supp. Fig. 3c: TMRCA bias under the wrong $N_e$). If $Q$ depends on those biased
$t$, the bias propagates. `--compress`'s span-weighted-average-then-reconcile age
smoothing ([§1](#sec-input)) adds a further calibration layer on top.

### 3.1 The order-only ablation — a measured *negative* result {#sec-ranked}

::: {.callout-warning}
## The hypothesised fix makes things worse. Do not use it for inference.

The order-only / ranked-topology variant ([`ranked_tree_sequence`](../api/ranked.ranked_tree_sequence.html))
was the natural robustness fix for the calibration risk — discard the (possibly
biased) time magnitudes, keep only coalescence *order*, in the spirit of Relate's
order-based selection test. **It does not work.** It is kept as a runnable
ablation only, never a recommended mode.
:::

The variant replaces each node's absolute time with the **dense rank** of that
time (order preserved, magnitudes discarded; topology and sample IDs unchanged, so
labels and truth transfer). MEASURED: it **collapses true-ARG painting from ~1.0
to ~0.50** (CLAUDE.md §6). The mechanism is a chain:

1. Dense-ranking **compresses the timescale**.
2. EM compensates by fitting a much **larger $Q$** (~$10^{-2}$ vs. ~$5\times10^{-5}$).
3. The large $Q$ **washes the deep/root branches**.
4. Washed roots make **$\pi$ unidentifiable** — it drifts to a degenerate extreme
   ([§3](#sec-pi)) → confident-wrong painting.

So the [§4](#sec-calibration) worry (mis-calibration hurts) was correct, but this
particular cure is wrong: it discards exactly the coalescence-depth **magnitudes**
the CTMC needs, and it worsens the $\pi$ degeneracy. The actual robustness fix for
the same $\pi$ failure is `estimate_pi=False` ([§3](#sec-pi)), not order-only.

## 5. The mugration approximation {#sec-mugration}

::: {.callout-warning}
## Genealogy and ancestry are *jointly* distributed; `tspaint` treats the genealogy as fixed

`tspaint` models ancestry as a discrete character evolving on a **fixed**
genealogy (the "mugration" / discrete-phylogeography construction,
[Lemey et al., 2009](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1000520)).
This ignores that the genealogy and the ancestry are not independent: under the
structured coalescent, **lineages in different demes cannot coalesce**, so the
tree shape itself carries ancestry information that the mugration model throws
away. The consequence is a **bias in the estimated $Q$**.
:::

This is a deliberate v1 simplification, stated openly. The structured-coalescent
methods that correct it —
[BASTA](https://journals.plos.org/plosgenetics/article?id=10.1371/journal.pgen.1005421)
([De Maio et al., 2015](https://journals.plos.org/plosgenetics/article?id=10.1371/journal.pgen.1005421)),
[MASCOT](https://academic.oup.com/mbe/article/35/11/2613/5072866)
([Müller et al., 2018](https://academic.oup.com/mbe/article/35/11/2613/5072866)),
and, on inferred ARGs specifically,
[SCAR](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1010422)
([Guo et al., 2022](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1010422))
— solve the joint genealogy↔ancestry problem, but their deliverable is migration
demography (rates, $N_e$, ancestral lineage locations), **not** per-haplotype
painting with credibility. For a v1 painting tool the naive mugration version is
likely fine in practice; the limitation is that the learned $Q$ should be read as
an effective ancestral-migration timescale, not an unbiased structured-coalescent
rate.

## 6. Loss of signal at old admixture {#sec-old-admixture}

::: {.callout-note}
## A coalescent limit, not a tree-inference one

At *old* admixture under present-day sampling, accuracy decays even on the **true
ARG**. This is not tree-inference error — it is the genealogy itself losing the
information.
:::

The headline hypothesis is that a tree-native method can reach **older** admixture
than tract-/copying-based methods (RFMix, MOSAIC, FLARE), because the genealogical
relationship of a query to its source references survives even after recombination
shreds the surrounding haplotype. The binding constraint becomes **tree accuracy**
rather than tract length. That is largely borne out — but with a measured ceiling.

MEASURED, discrimination vs. admixture age on a **true ARG** (deep split so only
tract length varies; balanced 50/50 admixture; balanced accuracy / mean
confidence; CLAUDE.md §9):

| $T_{\text{admix}}$ (generations) | balanced accuracy | mean confidence |
|:--|:--|:--|
| 30 | 0.99 | 0.72 |
| 300 | 0.93 | 0.36 |
| 1000 | 0.50 | 0.15 |
| 3000 | 0.50 | 0.18 |

`tspaint` discriminates well at recent-to-moderate admixture and **loses the
reference signal at old admixture**. Crucially, at $T_{\text{admix}} = 1000$ this
is *not* tree-inference error (the ARG is true) but **coalescent structure**:
admixed individuals sampled at the present coalesce **among themselves** before the
old pulse, which severs the query→reference genealogical link the method depends
on. This refines — does not retract — the headline: tract length is not the binding
constraint, but the query↔reference link is, and that link decays with admixture
age under present-day admixed sampling. The crossover age is scenario-specific (it
scales with $N_e$ and the sampling scheme); the qualitative loss-at-old is robust.

::: {.callout-note}
## Reporting note: report balanced accuracy + confidence, not plain accuracy

On a lopsided truth, plain accuracy is misleading — taking `argmax` of a posterior
near $P=0.5$ can score high while carrying no real information. Use
[`validate.balanced_accuracy`](../api/validate.balanced_accuracy.html) together
with [`validate.mean_confidence`](../api/validate.mean_confidence.html), as the
tables above do.
:::

## 7. Inherited from the front end: ARG accuracy is the binding constraint {#sec-arg-bound}

Even setting aside old-admixture coalescent limits, painting on an **inferred** ARG
is bounded by the ARG's quality. MEASURED on a `tsinfer`-inferred ARG (balanced
50/50, chance 0.5; CLAUDE.md §9): per-base accuracy ~0.53 at ~650 sites (poor ARG,
near chance), ~0.64 at ~2100 sites, ~0.88 at ~5400 sites (good ARG). Tree accuracy,
not tract length, is the binding constraint — exactly as predicted. The practical
implication: feed `tspaint` the best ARG you can. A Bayesian SMC sampler such as
SINGER ([`io.singer_tree_sequences`](../api/io.singer_tree_sequences.html)) largely
lifts this bound, and averaging across a *posterior* ensemble
([`ensemble.merge_posterior_tables`](../api/ensemble.merge_posterior_tables.html))
turns the residual ARG uncertainty into a calibrated band rather than a hidden
error.

## Summary

| Concern | Status | Mitigation / default |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Cross-tree node-ID stability | Required premise | `--compress` or tsinfer; run the [persistence check](#sec-persistence) first |
| Label-switching / $w_i$ / $Q$ collapse | Structural (soft mode) | Hard-clamped anchor set; $\mathrm{Beta}$ prior near 1; never float the whole panel |
| $\pi$ drift on washed roots | **Measured failure** | `estimate_pi=False` (default); uniform $\pi$ is principled |
| Panmictic branch-length bias | Real; biases $Q$ | Order-only ablation **does not help** (measured); feed well-calibrated times (SINGER) |
| Mugration approximation | Deliberate v1 simplification | Read $Q$ as an effective timescale; BASTA/MASCOT/SCAR correct it |
| Old admixture under present sampling | Coalescent limit | None — report balanced accuracy + confidence honestly |
| Inferred-ARG quality | Binding constraint | Best available ARG; SINGER posterior + ensemble merge |

The throughline: `tspaint`'s honesty *is* its selling point relative to black-box
ARG-native classifiers. It produces a calibrated soft posterior, a readable $Q$,
and explicit per-tip credibility — and, in the regimes where the signal is gone, it
relaxes toward $\pi$ rather than making a confident wrong call.
